title: Tiruchi Means a Turning-Point Conference, Does It Not? (6)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The printed source identifies this letter as 3558, and that number is preserved without normalising it from sequence. The OCR omitted the printed salutation and severely corrupted the quoted Tirukkural; both are restored here from the source images. Other repairs in the Tamil reading copy are limited to source-confirmed or unambiguous OCR errors and page-break fractures. The canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged.
paragraphs: Until last week, I had written about the DMK's fifth general State conference held in December 1975. After that came the DMK special conference in Madurai in May 1977; the Dravidar Kazhagam anti-Hindi conference in Chennai in August 1978; the DMK State anti-Hindi conference in Tiruchi that December; the sixth Tirunelveli District DMK conference in 1980; the first Periyar District DMK conference at Erode that December; the sixth North Arcot District DMK conference in the final week of January 1981; the sixth South Arcot District DMK conference at Cuddalore that July; the second State conference of the DMK Women's Wing at Tiruchi's Devar Hall that August; the sixth Thanjavur District DMK conference in May 1982; the East Ramanathapuram District DMK conference in July 1983; the Puducherry State DMK conference at the end of December 1984; the sixth Tiruchi District DMK conference at Karur in January 1984; the sixth Madurai District DMK conference at the end of that month; the sixth Salem District DMK conference that February; the sixth Anna District DMK conference that September; the State conference of the DMK Farmers' Wing in June 1986; and the DMK State conference against Hindi domination in Coimbatore that November. After all these, the DMK's sixth general State conference was held here in Tiruchi's Anna Nagar on 9, 10 and 11 February 1990. Its pavilion bore the name of the self-respect torch M. R. Krishnamurthy, beloved father of today's Cuddalore District Secretary, younger brother M. R. K. Panneerselvam; its hall bore the name of Thu. Pa. Azhagamuthu, who served with distinction as Chief Whip of the Tamil Nadu Government and whom I affectionately call "Zamindar."

Held alongside it, the Youth Wing's Conference for Socialist Social Reform and Renaissance ran from the morning until night on the second day. A vast procession took place on the first day. It was just before this sixth general State conference in Tiruchi that the description "turning point" first arose. Even reporters asked me: "You keep saying 'turning point.' What is this turning point?"

For the second State conference held in the same Tiruchi, Perarignar Anna had continuously written under the title "Anbil Invites," blending affection with classical Tamil, awakening movement comrades and drawing them to the conference. Likewise, when people ask why I declare under the title "Tiruchi Means a Turning-Point Conference, Does It Not?" that a State conference is a turning point, my answer is the one I gave at the sixth State conference itself.

"Suppose we set out from Tiruchi intending to reach Salem. To go to Salem, we may turn left along the way at Musiri and travel through Namakkal. But if we miss the turn at Musiri and continue all the way to Ulundurpet, we may then say: 'Oh, we have come as far as Ulundurpet, but were we not supposed to go to Salem? Never mind; there is a road from here to Salem, so let us take that route.' We can then travel from Ulundurpet to Salem. In the same way, our movement began in Tiruchi saying that we would march politically. But it failed to turn at Musiri, neglected its concern for social reform and went straight as far as Ulundurpet. The turning point is to act after recognising once again that our destination is Salem and that we must not abandon the principles of social reform!" This is what I explained at that sixth general State conference. I repeat it now.

Social reform and social renaissance have never been abandoned in the DMK's political journey. Let me recount an incident from the era of the Justice Party. About ninety years ago, a banquet was held at the King's palace in London. Wealthy men and nobles had assembled, all watching the route by which King George V would enter. The King arrived and everyone applauded. He came to each person and shook hands. After shaking hands with Sir A. T. Panneerselvam, Sir A. Ramasamy Mudaliar, Jayakar, Mohammed Usman and others, he approached a man with a curled moustache, broad forehead and open chest. When the King came to shake his hand, the man drew it back instead of extending it.

When the startled King looked at him, the man declared in a ringing voice in English: "Your Majesty! You must not touch me. If you touch me, harm will come to the King, because I belong to the Paraiyar community, one of India's untouchable classes." The astonished King immediately asked, "What justice is there in saying that one human being must not touch another?" Looking closely, he saw a card fastened to the man's coat that read: "Rao Bahadur R. Srinivasan, Paraiyan, I am an untouchable."

The King then said, "Never mind, Mr Rao Bahadur Srinivasan," firmly grasped his hand and shook it. This truly happened. Who was that Srinivasan? Born in Chengalpattu district as the son of a man named Rettaimalai, he joined his father's name to his own and called himself "Rettaimalai Srinivasan." That great man was one of the leaders of the Justice Party! Such was the extent to which untouchability ruled triumphantly in Tamil Nadu at that time.

It was this Rettaimalai Srinivasan who introduced and secured the passage of the "Resolution Against Untouchability" in the Madras Legislative Council in 1924. The government accepted it. I was born on 3 June 1924, the very day that resolution was introduced and passed. I have now crossed the age of ninety.

Reception Committee Chairman and younger brother Nehru has an irrepressible desire to bring me in advance and show me the immense arrangements for this conference. Had I been his age, would I not have come to see them without waiting for an invitation? I still have not forgotten the sight of Perarignar Anna arriving a full week before the 1956 State conference in Tiruchi, staying there, gathering the city's comrades, binding them together with the thread of affection and drawing work from them. That is why, even though I could not come, I sent the movement's Treasurer, younger brother M. K. Stalin, twice so that you would receive the same encouragement as if I had come, and I learned from him how the arrangements were progressing.

Last week, I reported all the funds that various district units had voluntarily contributed for this tenth State conference in Tiruchi. Following what I wrote, many more district secretaries went to Tiruchi, delighted in seeing all the arrangements, and contributed funds as well.

For the Tiruchi State conference, Labour Progressive Federation President Perur A. Natarajan, General Secretary M. Shanmugam, Treasurer K. Natarajan and other office-bearers met me in Chennai and presented fifteen lakh rupees on behalf of the Federation. Karur K. C. Palanisamy, MLA, contributed twenty-five lakh; Pudukkottai Raghupathi, twenty-five lakh; Karur Chinnasamy, twenty-five lakh; Ponmudy, on behalf of Villupuram District, fifteen lakh; Tha. Mo. Anbarasan, on behalf of Kanchipuram District, fifteen lakh; Sivalingam, on behalf of Salem District, five lakh; Saminathan, on behalf of Tiruppur District, ten lakh; Pongalur Palanisamy, on behalf of Coimbatore District, fifteen lakh; Periyakaruppan, on behalf of Sivaganga District, fifteen lakh; Periyannan Arasu, on behalf of Pudukkottai District, ten lakh; Nanniyur Rajendran, on behalf of Karur District, twenty lakh; J. Anbazhagan, on behalf of South Chennai District, ten lakh; Palanimanickam, on behalf of Thanjavur District, fifteen lakh; Soundira Pandian, MLA, on behalf of the Lalgudi-Pullambadi unit, twenty lakh; Farmers' Wing Secretary K. P. Ramalingam, on behalf of that wing, five lakh and forty thousand; area secretary Mathivanan, five lakh; union secretary Kathirvelu, five lakh; and many others also made contributions.

For our movement, it is customary at every conference to state openly how much revenue came in because of it, how much was spent, and what sum remained. When the first State conference was held in Chennai in 1951, the surplus was thirty thousand rupees. At the second State conference in 1956, the surplus was one lakh and ten thousand. At the third State conference, organised by friend Muthu in Madurai in 1961, the surplus was one lakh. When the fourth State conference was held in Chennai in 1967, I was Treasurer; including the amount I spent a year and a half travelling around to collect, the surplus was eleven lakh. At the fifth conference in Coimbatore, organised under the leadership of friend Punjai Puliampatti P. A. Saminathan, the surplus was ten lakh and ten thousand rupees.
When the DMK's sixth State conference was held in Tiruchi in 1990 with younger brother Selvaraj as Reception Committee Chairman, the surplus was 23,15,413 rupees.

When the DMK's seventh general State conference was held in Coimbatore in 1993, the surplus was thirty-one lakh rupees. When the eighth general State conference was held here in Tiruchi in 1996 with younger brother K. N. Nehru as Reception Committee Chairman, total conference collections were 1 crore, 32 thousand and 530 rupees. At this tenth general State conference in February 2014, younger brother K. N. Nehru is serving as Reception Committee Chairman for the third time, what people call a "hat trick." Like everyone else, I too am waiting to learn how large a surplus he will present.

The DMK's seventh general State conference and anti-Hindi conference was held in Coimbatore on 26, 27 and 28 March 1993, with our M. Kannappan as Reception Committee Chairman. It took place at Perarignar Anna Nagar, in the Thalamuthu Natarajan pavilion, with the Valparai Ramasamy entrance and the Coimbatore Aranganathan hall.

If Coimbatore and its surroundings are industrial regions, Tiruchi, where the tenth State conference will be held, and the surrounding districts are the Cauvery delta, districts filled with farmers. It will be a conference where the great agricultural community gathers rank upon rank.

Valluvar said: "Better than ploughing is manuring; after weeding, better than watering is guarding the crop." Applying manure is better than merely ploughing. And after manuring, watering and removing weeds, guarding the field so that cattle do not graze there is better than all of them, Valluvar said. In the same way, all of us are engaged in the precious work of protecting and nurturing our thousand-year crop, the DMK, which Anna, the farmer celebrated by the world, ploughed, weeded and cultivated with care. The DMK is the peerless guide of Tamil society! Its mighty protective fortress! Its dawn lamp! The clan lamp set upon the hill! The lamp Anna placed in my hands with immense trust in 1969 before he left us! Some cast predictions, wondering whether that lamp will be extinguished by the winds that blow from time to time. I remain vigilant every moment precisely so that it will not be extinguished. Is this an ordinary lamp? Is it not the beacon atop the tower, lit by all of you, the people of Tamil Nadu, with the oil poured by Thanthai Periyar and the wick placed by Perarignar Anna? We will gather in Tiruchi on the 15th to take a pledge: to build and protect this movement; to strengthen it further; to carry out its work without slackening; and, with heads held high, to continue our journey along the path of progress.

Explaining the conference arrangements to reporters, Reception Committee Chairman and younger brother Nehru said that the entrance had been designed like the Red Fort and the facade like Parliament. For the convenience of volunteers, arrangements include 550 toilets, bathing tanks at ten locations, purified drinking-water taps at forty locations, parking for ten thousand vehicles, an ambulance and a medical team. The list of speakers and their subjects has been prepared at party headquarters and will be announced through the newspapers in a day or two. No movement or political party anywhere in the world can have planned and conducted conferences on this scale as ours has. My dear Udanpirappugal, who have earned the distinction of turning every DMK conference into a victory conference, hurry towards Tiruchi and make this tenth State conference a victory conference too! I will keep my eyes fixed upon the road by which you come, waiting for you.
With affection,
M. K.
3 February 2014
